coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn-on Cu2-xSe@HA-rhodamine 6G FRET nanoprobe for hyaluronidase detection and imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Guojuan
Kong, Bo
Zhao, Jiaqiang
Ni, Huanhuan
Zhan, Lei
Huang, Chengzhi
Zou, Hongyan
coredata.dc:description: The application of nanostructures to design fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensing platforms has been greatly concerned with the demand for sensitive and selective detection of biomolecules. Here, a novel sensitive turn-on fluorescence strategy based on the FRET mechanism has been designed for hyaluronidase (HAase) detection through the modulation of Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoprobe fabricated by self-assembly of rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) together with Cu2-xSe@HA nanoparticles through electrostatic adsorption. The Cu2-xSe@HA had extensive localized surface plasma resonance (LSPR) absorption in the wide range of ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths and showed good light capture characteristics, which can be acted as good acceptors in the FRET interactions with Rh6G, inducing its efficient fluorescence quenching. In the presence of HAase, the FRET process was disrupted and the fluorescence signal was recovered. In the range of 0.1–10.0 U/mL, the fluorescence recovery of Rh6G showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of HAase, and the detection limit was 0.06 U/mL. The sensing platform has been used for HAase detection in real urine samples and cancer cells imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Cu2-xSe@HA
Localized surface plasma resonance (LSPR)
Hyaluronidase
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental Conditions Optimization for HAase Detection
Performance of the Proposed Sensing System
Determination of HAase in Real Samples
Application of the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G Nanoprobe in Cell Imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (Grant No. cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0873 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 21705130 , 21535006 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs. (B) Particle size distribution histograms (one hundred nanoparticles were counted). (C) The HRTEM image of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs; (D) The absorption spectra of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs (inset: the photograph of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs dispersion solution).
(A) Full-scan XPS profiles of the Cu2-xSe@HA NPs. (B) High resolution spectra for Cu 2p. (C) Cu LMM. (D) High resolution spectra for Se 3d.

(A) The normalized absorption spectra of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs (black line), excitation spectra (blue line) and emission spectra (read line) of Rh6G. (B) The extinction spectra of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs, Rh6G, Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G and Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G + HAase. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of Rh6G, Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G and Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G + HAase, λex = 450 nm. [Rh6G] = 2.0 μM, [Cu2-xSe@HA NPs] = 250 μg/mL, [HAase] = 8.0 U/mL (≈176 nM). (D) The changes of fluorescence lifetime of Rh6G (λex = 375 nm, λem = 565 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G incubated with different concentrations of HAase (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, 6.0, 8.0, 10.0, 15.0 and 20.0 U/mL). (B) The change of fluorescence with the increasing HAase concentration. The inset showed the linear relationship between the fluorescence and concentrations of HAase within the range of 0.1–10.0 U/mL.

Selectivity of the sensing system for the detection of HAase. HSA (50 nM), Urease (100 nM), Try (100 nM), Lys (200 nM), Thr (200 nM), Cys (200 nM), GSH (200 nM), Glu (200 nM), Gln (200 nM), Urea (200 nM), UA (200 nM), NaCl (200 nM), KCl (200 nM), and HAase (8.0 U/mL).
Cellular toxicity of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs and Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G on HeLa cells.
Fluorescence images of L02 cells and HeLa cells with/without free HA, incubating with the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G (40 μg/mL) for 3 h.
Schematic illustration of HAase detection by the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoprobe.
Comparison of different methods for HAase detection.
Determination of HAase in human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn-on Cu2-xSe@HA-rhodamine 6G FRET nanoprobe for hyaluronidase detection and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analysis System, Chongqing Science and Technology Commission, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Beibei, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analysis System, Chongqing Science and Technology Commission, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analysis System, Chongqing Science and Technology Commission, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Beibei, Chongqing, 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of nanostructures to design fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensing platforms has been greatly concerned with the demand for sensitive and selective detection of biomolecules. Here, a novel sensitive turn-on fluorescence strategy based on the FRET mechanism has been designed for hyaluronidase (HAase) detection through the modulation of Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoprobe fabricated by self-assembly of rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) together with Cu2-xSe@HA nanoparticles through electrostatic adsorption. The Cu2-xSe@HA had extensive localized surface plasma resonance (LSPR) absorption in the wide range of ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths and showed good light capture characteristics, which can be acted as good acceptors in the FRET interactions with Rh6G, inducing its efficient fluorescence quenching. In the presence of HAase, the FRET process was disrupted and the fluorescence signal was recovered. In the range of 0.1–10.0 U/mL, the fluorescence recovery of Rh6G showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of HAase, and the detection limit was 0.06 U/mL. The sensing platform has been used for HAase detection in real urine samples and cancer cells imaging.

An off-on fluorescence nanoprobe for hyaluronidase (HAase) has been developed through the modulation of FRET between plasmonic Cu2-xSe@HA and rhodamine 6G. And the proposed sensing platform is applicable for the sensitive detection of HAase in real urine samples and for the imaging of cancer cells with HAase overexpression.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is involved in the typical photochemical interaction between the donor and acceptor pairs [1]. In the spectral sense, the acceptor absorption receptor and the donor emission must overlap to some extent [2]. So, selecting suitable donor and acceptor pairs must be essential for the design of FRET based system. As most nanomaterials have a high specific surface area, wide absorption and large molar absorption coefficient, it is easy to make small molecules adsorb on their surface and shorten the distance between them, thus the efficient FRET can be realized [3,4]. In recent years, many nanomaterials have been used in FRET development such as quantum dots [5], gold nanoparticles [6], carbon dots [7], upconversion nanoparticles [8], et al. Nonstoichiometric copper selenide nanoparticles (Cu2-xSe NPs), as new p-type semiconductors, have been greatly concerned in catalysis [9], sensors [10] and medical therapies [11] for their typical localized surface plasma resonance (LSPR) properties. Because of their high copper vacancies, the extinction spectra of nonstoichiometric Cu2-xSe nanostructures can extend from ultraviolet (UV) to the near-infrared (NIR) [12,13]. So Cu2-xSe NPs can be selected as good FRET acceptors as their extinction can overlap with the emission of many donor fluorophores. Thus, the combination of Cu2-xSe NPs with fluorescent molecules to construct FRET based sensor is worthy of investigation.

Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a negative charged macromolecular mucopolysaccharide, which widely exists in the extracellular matrix and has good biocompatibility and biodegradability [14–16]. Hyaluronidase (HAase), a hydrolase that can hydrolyze HA into small molecular fragments is rich in many tumor matrices and tumor lysosomes [17,18]. High contents of HAase can accelerate the growth of cancer cells [19], such as bladder [20], brain [21], and breast cancer [22]. The overexpression of HAase in urine and serum of cancer patients have been proved in previous studies, and HAase is regarded as a potential tumor marker [23,24]. So, developing a high-sensitivity method for HAase detection in tumor diagnosis is very urgent and meaningful.

In this work, Cu2-xSe nanostructures and rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) were chosen as the FRET acceptor and donor pairs to create the novel FRET system for HAase detection and imaging. As shown in Scheme 1 , HA capped Cu2-xSe nanoparticles (Cu2-xSe@HA NPs) were synthesized by typical hydrothermal methods using negatively charged HA as a stabilizer. The Rh6G can be immobilized on the surface of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs easily through electrostatic adsorption as well as their large areas to fabricate the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoassembly, in which Rh6G as signal reporters and Cu2-xSe@HA NPs as fluorescence quencher for their short distance and good spectral overlaps. When the HAase was introduced into the system, the HA can be specifically hydrolyzed into small molecular fragments inducing the FRET process disturbed, and then the fluorescence of Rh6G was recovered. Thus, a novel sensitive turn-on fluorescence strategy based on the FRET mechanism has been developed for HAase detection in real urine samples and cancer cell imaging due to the specific targeting of HA to cluster determinant 44 (CD44).

HA was supplied by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. HAase (400–1000 U/mg) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Selenium dioxide (SeO2), ascorbic acid (Vc) and Rh6G were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Copper sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O) and Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. and Ruixin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. respectively.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra of samples were acquired by a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) and a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). A transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyzer (Talos F200X, FEI Company, USA) and an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyzer (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo, USA) have been used for nanoprobe morphological characterization. A DSU live-cell confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan) system was used for cell fluorescent imaging and image processing. The fluorescence TCSPC spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France) was used for fluorescence lifetime measuring. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a D8 ADVANCE (BRUCKER, Germany).

The method for the preparation of Cu2-xSe@HA nanoparticles was improved on the basis of reference [25]. 0.2 mL SeO2(0.05 M)was mixed with 0.8 mL HA (1.0 mg/mL) and 0.4 mL Vc (15 mg/mL) for 10 min. Then, 0.4 mL CuSO4·5H2O (0.1 M) and 0.8 mL Vc (15 mg/mL) was added. The reaction mixtures remained vigorous stirring at 30 °C for 30 min, then heated to 45 °C and continued to react for 10 h at 25 °C. Finally, the product solution which was dark green was centrifuged for 10 min (10,000 rpm) and stored for later use.

Different concentrations of HAase were added to the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G solutions (PBS buffer, pH 5.8, 2.0 μM Rh6G, 250 μg/mL Cu2-xSe@HA). And the fluorescence was measured after incubation at 37 °C for 90 min.

Three urine samples from three healthy people were prepared. The urine samples were collected and centrifuged for 20 min (10,000 rpm), and the upper supernatant solution was used for further detection. Briefly, 100 μL of urine samples were mixed with Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G and PBS buffer solution (pH 5.8), then the fluorescence of the samples was measured according to the aforementioned method. Besides that, the HAase concentrations of the same urine samples were measured using a commercial ELISA kit for comparison.

The HeLa cells (1.0 × 105 cells/mL) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. After addition with different concentrations of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs or Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G (0, 20, 40, 80, 160, 200 and 250 μg/mL) respectively, the cells continued to be incubated for 24 h. Then, the cells were washed with PBS for three times, and incubated in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) for 30 min. After that, the optical absorption (at 450 nm) of each well was measured using a microplate reader (Bio-Tek, USA). Each group at least 6 parallel measurements were taken [26].

The HeLa cells and L02 cells (0.5 × 105 cells/mL) were seeded under the same conditions. After incubation with HA (2.5 mg/mL) for 30 min, Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G (40 μg/mL) were incubated with the HeLa cells and L02 cells for 3 h. Then, the medium was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS, and resupplied with fresh medium. Finally, they were imaged under the confocal microscope.

The obtained Cu2-xSe@HA NPs by the hydrothermal synthesis were well-dispersed and quasi-spherical about 105.0 nm in diameter (Fig. 1A, B). It can be observed that the HA was enveloped on the surface of Cu2-xSe cores. The HA acted as the surface-active agent to help nucleation and there existed damping effect of the HA on the successfully prepared Cu2-xSe particles (Fig. S1). The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1C) showed that the interlayer lattice distance of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs was 0.20 nm which corresponded to the (220) plane of cubic Cu2-xSe [10]. Besides, the XRD showed that the major diffraction peaks of the Cu2-xSe@HA (111, 220, 311, 400, 422) matched well with the pure cubic phase of Cu2-xSe (JCPDS NO.06–0680). And the broad peak at 25° was derived from the amorphous nature of HA (Fig. S2). The Cu2-xSe@HA NPs showed a wide extinction spectrum in the UV to NIR range and a typical NIR LSPR peak (Fig. 1D) for their high carrier density (copper vacancy), which was consistent with the experimental report [27]. Furthermore, the nanoparticles had good stability in water (Fig. S3).

As shown in Fig. 2A, the full-scan XPS profiles confirmed the C, N, O, Se and Cu elements existed in the nanostructures prepared, where N came from the HA. And the atomic ratio of Cu/Se was 1.3:1 (x ≈ 0.7). From the high-resolution spectra for Cu 2p (Fig. 2B), two obvious peaks at 932.0 eV and 952.0 eV can be ascribed to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively, confirming the existence of Cu+. Meanwhile, the other two fitting peaks at 933.3 eV and 947.0 eV belonged to Cu2+ [28,29]. The auger Cu LMM spectrum (Fig. 2C) was further confirmed the existence of Cu2+ (568.7 eV) and Cu+ (569.7 eV) in Cu2-xSe@HA NPs [30]. For the high-resolution peaks at 54.2 eV and 55.0 eV of Se 3d (Fig. 2D), the existence of Se2− was confirmed [31].

The prepared Cu2-xSe@HA NPs displayed broad absorption in the range of UV to NIR wavelengths (Fig. 3A). The Rh6G was chosen as the FRET donor because of its high quantum yield and good emission overlapping with the absorption of prepared Cu2-xSe@HA NPs. When Rh6G was assembly with the Cu2-xSe@HA, the absorption of the nanomaterials complex showed both the characteristic peak of Rh6G at 525 nm and Cu2-xSe@HA NPs at 1073 nm (Fig. 3B), and the significant decrease of FL intensity of Rh6G could be observed (Fig. 3C), indicating the successful formation of Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoassembly, which can be further confirmed by appearance of CO characteristic peak at 1716 cm−1 and benzene ring characteristic peak at 1527 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1 for the IR spectra of Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G (Fig. S4). But in the presence of HAase, the characteristic absorption of Rh6G became clearer and the intensity of its fluorescence was enhanced. The decreased absorption of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs at 1073 nm was attributed to the degradation of HA by HAase, resulting in Cu2-xSe@HA NPs becoming unstable. These results confirmed that the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoassembly has the potential to be an “off-on” fluorescent probe used in HAase detection.

The good overlapping between the wide absorption of the prepared Cu2-xSe@HA and the Rh6G emission spectrum, and the shortened change in fluorescence lifetime (τ) of Rh6G with the addition of Cu2-xSe@HA confirmed the happening of FRET in the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoassembly (Fig. 3D). However, when HAase was added, the fluorescence lifetime of Rh6G recovered corresponding to the recovery of the fluorescence. These phenomena further proved the feasibility of modulating FRET sensing strategy for HAase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental Conditions Optimization for HAase Detection
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the purpose of sensitive detection of HAase, relevant conditions including concentrations of Cu2-xSe@HA NPs and Rh6G, reaction time and media pH values were optimized in detail. Firstly, the effects of the concentrations of Cu2-xSe@HA and Rh6G were examined in the range of 75–350 μg/mL and 1.0 μM–3.5 μM, respectively, and the results showed that the optimal concentrations were 250 μg/mL and 2.0 μM (Fig. S5 and S6). Then, we further studied the effect of reaction time on the sensing system, and it can be used for 90 min (Fig. S7). Finally, the PBS buffer solutions with varying pH values were examined and the results revealed that the optimal pH was obtained at pH 5.8 (Fig. S8). In conclusion, the 250 μg/mL Cu2-xSe@HA NPs, 2.0 μM Rh6G, 90 min and pH 5.8 were used as the optimal conditions of the following analytical experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the Proposed Sensing System
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions, with the addition of different concentrations of HAase (0–20.0 U/mL), the quenched Rh6G emission by Cu2-xSe@HA NPs can be gradually recovered, and the fluorescence intensity reached a plateau when HAase exceeded 10.0 U/mL (Fig. 4A). It can be seen that the fluorescent ratio of (F
F 0)/F 0 had a linear relationship as (F
F 0)/F 0 = 0.082[HAase] U/mL + 0.086 (R 2 = 0.9956) with the HAase concentrations ranging from 0.1–10.0 U/mL (Fig. 4B). The detection limit calculated based on the 3δ/k was 0.06 U/mL. Compared with some other reports (Table 1 ), the current method had relatively better sensitivity for HAase detection. In addition，the intermediate precision of intra-day and inter-day was evaluated. As shown in Table S1, three different concentrations of HAase (1.0, 4.0, 8.0 U/mL) were analyzed in the sensing system in three days. The R.S.D. (0.24% − 4.01%) values showed that the proposed method had good precision.

Various potential interferences such as inorganic salts: NaCl and KCl, proteins: human serum albumin (HSA), Trypsin (Try) and Urease, amino acids: Lysine (Lys), Threonine (Thr) and Cysteine (Cys), glucose (Glu), Glutamine (Gln), glutathione(GSH), Uric acid (UA) and Urea were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 , under the same experimental conditions, the addition of these substances led to no interference to the detection signal of the HAase. All these results confirmed that the nanoprobe exhibited good selectivity and it had the potential for HAase detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HAase in Real Samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three urine samples from healthy people were analyzed using the developed method. For comparison, the commercial ELISA kit was also used. As shown in Table 2 , the concentrations of HAase determined in urine samples by this method were basically consistent with that by ELISA kit. The above results indicated that the practicability of the sensing strategy in the complex matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G Nanoprobe in Cell Imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the Cu2-xSe@HA and Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G were investigated using the HeLa cells, and both of the Cu2-xSe@HA and Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G showed little cytotoxicity in a wide concentration range (20–160 μg/mL, Fig. 6 ). The toxicity of the nanoparticle at certain concentrations (200–250 μg/mL) may be caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS), which produced by Cu-catalyzed Fenton-like reactions induced by overexpressed hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in tumor sites [36]. Besides, the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G exhibited good stability to DNase I (Deoxyribonuclease I) (Fig. S9). The results indicated the possibility of this method for bioimaging application. CD44, a transmembrane glycoprotein, is usually overexpressed on tumor cells such as lung and breast cancer [37]. As the major receptor of HA, CD44 shows a good affinity for HA and can colocalize with HA in the same cells [38]. Therefore, tumor cells of express CD44 receptors are capable of promoting the binding and internalization of HA-based nanoparticles [39–41].

To examine the prepared nanoprobe for the targeting ability and imaging in cancer cells, the normal human cell line (L02 cells with the low expression level of CD44) and the human cancer line (HeLa cells with the high expression level of CD44) were used for confocal imaging. When the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoprobe was incubated with HeLa cells, the fluorescence recovery of Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G appeared after 3 h incubation (Fig. 7 ). This phenomenon demonstrated that the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoprobe had internalized into HeLa cells and then hydrolyzed by HAase which was overexpressed in the cytosol of cells, leading to the disintegration of nanoprobe and the fluorescence recovery of Rh6G. In contrast, no significant fluorescence recovery was observed after the same incubation with L02 cells. Moreover, free HA (2.5 mg/mL) was first added into the HeLa cells to block CD44. As expected, only weak fluorescence can be observed. This result implied that the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoprobe can selectively bind to CD44 through HA and then targeting at cancer cells. All the above results showed that the Cu2-xSe@HA-Rh6G nanoprobe can be used for targeting cancer cell imaging.

In summary, Cu2-xSe@HA NPs and Rh6G have been chosen as a novel FRET donor /acceptor pair to design a turn-on fluorescence strategy for HAase detection. Cu2-xSe@HA NPs served as excellent energy transfer acceptors and fluorescence quenchers for their intense and broad absorption over UV to NIR wavelengths, which spectrally overlap with the emission of Rh6G. With the addition of HAase, the FRET process was disrupted and the fluorescence signal was recovered. The proposed sensing strategy not only was used to detect HAase in human urine samples, but also has been used in the imaging of cancer cells with HAase overexpression, which revealed great potential for copper chalcogenides with practical applications in biomedical fields.

Guojuan Yan: Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Bo Kong: Methodology, Data curation. Jiaqiang Zhao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Huanhuan Ni: Formal analysis. Lei Zhan: Conceptualization, Supervision. Chengzhi Huang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hongyan Zou: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (Grant No. cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0873) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 21705130, 21535006).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scharateriation of the nanomaterails (Fig. S1-S4) and the experimental conditions optimization (Fig. S5-S8) are available in the supplementary matrial.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2022.112496.
